turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion of the Court in case 14-1538, Life Technologies versus Promega Corporation.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: This case comes to us from the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit. We grant this certiorari to resolve whether the supply of a single component of a multi-component invention is in an infringing act under §271(f)(1) of the Patent Act. We hold that it is not. Promega Corporation was the exclusive licensee of a patent claiming a toolkit for genetic testing. Promega sublicensed the patent to Life Technologies for the manufacture and sale of the kits for use in certain licensed law enforcement work worldwide. The patented kit contains five components, all but one of which Life Technologies manufactured in the United Kingdom. That single component, an enzyme referred to as the Taq polymerase was manufactured in the United States and then shipped to Life Technologies' United Kingdom facility where it was combined with the other four components of the kit. Promega sued Life Technologies on the ground that Life Technologies had infringed the patent by selling the kits outside the licensed fields of use. Promega alleged that liability was triggered under §271(f)(1) of the Patent Act which prohibits the supply from the United States of all or a substantial portion of the components of a patented invention for combination abroad. The jury returned a verdict for Promega, but the district court granted Life Technologies' motion for judgment as a matter of law, holding that §271(f)(1)'s phrase, “all or a substantial portion” did not encompass the supply of a single component of a multi-component invention. The Federal Circuit reversed because it determined that a single important component like the enzyme could constitute a substantial portion of the components of the invention. We now reverse the judgment of the Federal Circuit. Although the ordinary meaning of the term “substantial portion” may refer either to a qualitative importance or to a quantitatively large size, the statutory text, context and structure of §271(f) points to a quantitative measure that moreover must be more than a single commodity component. For instance, the words neighboring the term, “all” and “portion,” convey a quantitative meaning and §271(f)(1)'s consistent use of the plural, “components,” indicates that multiple components make up the substantial portion, not just a single component, no matter its relative importance. A qualitative meaning, on the other hand, would render the modifying phrase, “of the components” unnecessary the the first time it is used in §271(f)(1). Further, reading §271(f)(1) to cover any single component would leave little room for §271(f)(2) which refers to, “any component” and would undermine §271(f)(2)'s express reference to a single component especially made or especially adapted for use in the invention. The better reading allows the two provisions to work in tandem and gives each provision its own unique application. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we hold that a single component does not constitute a substantial portion of the components that can give rise to liability under §271(f)(1). The judgment of the Federal Circuit is reversed and the case is remanded for further proceedings. Justice Alito has filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment in which Justice Thomas joins. The Chief Justice took no part in the decision of this case.
